text: You have held a belief about a colleague for three years. A trusted friend presents evidence that directly contradicts it. What is your first response?
opt_a_text: Defend the original belief - one counter-example does not overturn years of experience.
opt_b_text: Ignore the evidence for now and return to it when you have more time.
opt_c_text: Investigate further before changing your position - but remain genuinely open.
opt_d_text: Accept the new evidence immediately and update your view.

text: You realise midway through a project that your initial assumptions were wrong. What do you do?
opt_a_text: Continue as planned - changing direction now would waste effort already invested.
opt_b_text: Make small adjustments but avoid publicly acknowledging the error.
opt_c_text: Pause the project, communicate the error clearly, and recalibrate.
opt_d_text: Quietly fix what you can without drawing attention to the mistake.

text: A problem affects your family but it is not technically your fault. What is your response?
opt_a_text: Wait for the responsible party to address it.
opt_b_text: Complain about the situation to others.
opt_c_text: Ask what role you could play in solving it, even if it was not your fault.
opt_d_text: Distance yourself to avoid being seen as responsible.

text: A colleague fails to complete a task that was part of a shared project you are leading. What do you do?
opt_a_text: Report the failure to management immediately.
opt_b_text: Take ownership of the gap and address it while finding out what happened.
opt_c_text: Leave it - it was their responsibility, not yours.
opt_d_text: Ask the colleague to explain themselves publicly.

text: Two people witness the same event and reach completely different conclusions. What is the most likely explanation?
opt_a_text: One of them must be lying.
opt_b_text: Memory is selective and interpretation shapes what we retain.
opt_c_text: Facts are subjective - there is no objective truth.
opt_d_text: One of them is simply more intelligent.

text: A manager consistently assigns the most complex tasks to one employee without explanation. What is the most useful interpretation?
opt_a_text: The employee is being overloaded unfairly.
opt_b_text: The manager trusts the employee more than the others.
opt_c_text: The situation requires more context before reaching any conclusion.

text: A person works very hard for 20 years but remains frustrated with their outcomes. Which question is most useful to ask first?
opt_c_text: Is their effort aligned with a clear sense of direction?

text: You are offered a promotion that pays significantly more but takes you away from work you find meaningful. What guides your decision?
opt_a_text: The salary - financial security enables everything else.
opt_b_text: A clear sense of what you are trying to build over the next ten years.
opt_c_text: What your peers would think is the smart choice.
opt_d_text: How difficult it would be to return to your current role if needed.

text: A company has been successful for 15 years and suddenly begins losing market share. What is the most likely explanation?
opt_b_text: The environment changed but the company did not recalibrate in time.

text: You notice that a successful person seems to do less in certain periods but achieve more. What does this suggest?
opt_a_text: They are not being honest about how hard they work.
opt_b_text: They have learned to read timing and context, not just effort.
opt_c_text: They have connections others do not have.
opt_d_text: Their industry is easier than yours.

text: A city cuts funding for sanitation services to reduce costs. What is the most likely long-term consequence?
opt_a_text: No major impact - people will adapt.
opt_b_text: Improved efficiency as the private sector fills the gap.
opt_c_text: Hidden societal costs will emerge over time - disease, inequality, civic breakdown.
opt_d_text: Greater motivation for citizens to take personal responsibility.

text: A university loses its Vice Chancellor for one week. A university loses all its janitors for one week. Which creates the larger immediate operational crisis?
opt_a_text: The Vice Chancellor - leadership is irreplaceable.
opt_b_text: The janitors - without functioning facilities, daily operations collapse.
opt_c_text: Equal - both are critical at different levels.
opt_d_text: Impossible to know without more context.

text: Two departments in an organisation have competing priorities and are in conflict. What is the most effective first step?
opt_a_text: Escalate to senior management to decide.
opt_c_text: Create a shared space to identify what both departments actually need, not just what they want.
opt_d_text: Document the conflict formally to protect your position.

text: You disagree strongly with a decision made by your team. What do you do?
opt_a_text: Accept the decision without comment - it is not your call.
opt_b_text: Object loudly until the team changes course.
opt_c_text: Express your disagreement clearly, explain your reasoning, then commit to the collective decision if it stands.
opt_d_text: Quietly undermine the decision by not fully implementing it.

text: A company identifies digital photography before everyone else but continues investing primarily in film. What is the core problem?
opt_a_text: Lack of knowledge about the new technology.
opt_b_text: Bad luck in the timing of the market shift.
opt_c_text: Poor placement of responsibility - the knowledge existed but the structure resisted change.
opt_d_text: The new technology was not yet reliable enough.

text: A colleague resists adopting a new digital tool that would save significant time. What is the most useful interpretation?
opt_a_text: They are not intelligent enough to learn new tools.
opt_b_text: The tool may not have been introduced in a way that made its value clear.
opt_c_text: They are afraid of change - a character flaw.
opt_d_text: Their resistance may reflect a deeper placement issue - wrong role, wrong context, wrong timing.
